choices.rationale: Incorrect: bladder distention was specifically excluded on exam and is an uncommon cause after a brief myringotomy with minimal fluids.

explanation: Emergence delirium is a transient state of disorientation, non-purposeful agitation, and inconsolability seen most often in children aged 2 to 6 years, particularly after sevoflurane or desflurane and short procedures with little intrinsic pain. It is a diagnosis of exclusion: hypoxia, pain, hypoglycemia, and bladder distention must be ruled out first. The condition is usually self-limited, but distressing or unsafe agitation is treated with dexmedetomidine, propofol, or fentanyl; intraoperative dexmedetomidine or propofol reduces its incidence.
subtopic: Postoperative complications (emergence delirium)

verdict.reason: Keyed answer C (emergence delirium; treat with dexmedetomidine or propofol) is the single best answer. The vignette is textbook ED: a preschooler (2-6 yr highest-risk group) after a sevoflurane mask anesthetic for a brief, minimally painful procedure (myringotomy is the classic example), with non-purposeful agitation, no caregiver recognition, inconsolability, and hypoxia/pain/bladder distention explicitly excluded. All distractors are genuinely inferior/incorrect: B (hypoxemia) is excluded by SpO2 99% on RA with no obstruction; D (LAST) is impossible since no local anesthetic was given; E (bladder) was specifically excluded on exam; A (pain/opioid) is the only somewhat plausible distractor but is correctly inferior because myringotomy is minimally painful and the agitation is non-purposeful — it misattributes the diagnosis as pain. Note the rationales for both C and A mention that ED itself responds to opioids/fentanyl; this is medically accurate (fentanyl is a recognized ED rescue agent) and does NOT create a two-correct-answers ambiguity, because A's stated diagnosis (pain) is wrong while C's diagnosis (ED) and listed treatments (dexmedetomidine/propofol) are correct. The explanation is accurate and current: ED as a diagnosis of exclusion, self-limited course, pharmacologic rescue with dexmedetomidine/propofol/fentanyl, and reduced incidence with intraoperative dexmedetomidine/propofol. REFERENCE CHECK: WebFetch of https://www.openanesthesia.org/keywords/emergence-delirium/ resolved to a genuinely on-topic OpenAnesthesia page that confirms every key claim — age 2-6 highest risk, sevoflurane > desflurane > isoflurane ordering, ENT/eye surgery predisposition, diagnosis often confounded by pain, and treatment doses dexmedetomidine 0.3-0.5 mcg/kg, propofol 0.5-1 mg/kg, fentanyl 1-2 mcg/kg, plus prevention being more effective than treatment. Reference is valid and topical. No errors found.

stem: A 68-year-old man is scheduled for an elective open right hemicolectomy for colon cancer. His history includes type 2 diabetes on insulin, hypertension, and a transient ischemic attack 3 years ago. He reports that he is able to walk on a flat surface and do light housework but becomes short of breath and has to stop after climbing one flight of stairs. His resting ECG shows nonspecific ST-T changes unchanged from a tracing 1 year ago, and he has no active cardiac symptoms. Vitals are stable. According to the ACC/AHA stepwise approach to perioperative cardiac evaluation, what is the most appropriate next step in his cardiac workup?

choices.text: Proceed to surgery without further cardiac testing
choices.rationale: Correct: his procedure carries elevated risk and his functional capacity is poor/indeterminate, but current guidelines recommend proceeding when further testing would not change perioperative management.

choices.text: Obtain pharmacologic stress testing before surgery
choices.rationale: Incorrect: stress testing is only indicated when it would change management, and there is no plan for revascularization purely to reduce noncardiac surgical risk here.

choices.text: Obtain a resting transthoracic echocardiogram to measure ejection fraction
choices.rationale: Incorrect: routine preoperative echocardiography is not indicated in a patient without signs or symptoms of new or worsening heart failure or valvular disease.

choices.text: Refer for coronary angiography with possible preoperative revascularization
choices.rationale: Incorrect: prophylactic coronary revascularization before noncardiac surgery does not improve outcomes and is reserved for patients who would need it independent of surgery.

choices.text: Cancel surgery and refer to cardiology for a 6-week optimization program
choices.rationale: Incorrect: cancellation is unwarranted for an oncologic resection in a stable patient when no intervention would alter perioperative management.

explanation: In the ACC/AHA stepwise algorithm, after confirming the absence of an acute coronary syndrome and estimating combined surgical/clinical risk, the decision to test hinges on whether functional capacity is adequate AND whether testing would change management. Even though this patient has poor functional capacity (<4 METs) and an elevated Revised Cardiac Risk Index, additional noninvasive testing is recommended only if the result would alter perioperative care. Because neither revascularization nor cancellation is indicated for this stable patient, the correct step is to proceed to surgery with appropriate medical optimization and monitoring.
subtopic: Preoperative cardiac risk stratification and functional capacity assessment
reference_title: Preoperative Cardiac Risk Stratification and Evaluation - OpenAnesthesia

verdict.reason:
The keyed answer ("proceed to surgery without further cardiac testing") is the single best answer. The stem is deliberately constructed to land on the ACC/AHA decision node where a patient has elevated MACE risk (RCRI elevated: DM on insulin, prior TIA/cerebrovascular disease, intraperitoneal high-risk surgery) plus poor/indeterminate functional capacity (stops after one flight of stairs, ~<4 METs), but with no ACS, no active symptoms, and an unchanged ECG. At that node the guideline gate is explicitly conditional: noninvasive/stress testing is pursued ONLY if the result would change management. Since neither prophylactic revascularization nor cancellation is indicated for this stable patient, testing would not change management, so proceeding is correct.

Distractors are all genuinely inferior and their rationales are accurate: (B) stress testing only when it alters management — correct limiting principle; (C) routine preop echo not indicated absent signs/symptoms of new/worsening HF or valvular disease — correct; (D) prophylactic coronary revascularization before noncardiac surgery does not improve outcomes and is reserved for standalone indications (CARP trial) — correct and current; (E) cancelling an oncologic resection in a stable patient when no intervention would change management is unwarranted — correct.

The explanation is medically accurate and consistent with both the 2014 and 2024 ACC/AHA perioperative frameworks (test only if functional capacity is inadequate AND testing changes management). No two-defensible-answer ambiguity: the stem engineers away the conditions that would make stress testing the answer.

REFERENCE CHECK: WebFetched https://www.openanesthesia.org/keywords/preoperative-cardiac-risk-stratification-and-evaluation/ — it resolves and is genuinely on-topic. The page covers the ACC/AHA stepwise algorithm, RCRI, functional capacity/METs (poor = <4 METs), stress testing indications, and explicitly states that patients with poor/unknown functional status are evaluated "if further testing will change decision-making or perioperative care," directly supporting the keyed answer. No fabricated/irrelevant reference.

stem: During a preoperative airway examination of a 52-year-old woman scheduled for thyroidectomy, you ask her to sit upright, open her mouth maximally, and protrude her tongue without phonating. You can see the soft palate and the base of the uvula, but the uvular tip and the tonsillar pillars are obscured by the tongue. She has a thyromegaly, a thyromental distance of 5 cm, and full neck extension. Which modified Mallampati class best describes this airway view, and what is its general significance?

choices.text: Class I — full view of the soft palate, uvula, and tonsillar pillars; reassuring for easy laryngoscopy
choices.rationale: Incorrect: Class I requires visualization of the entire uvula and pillars, which are obscured in this patient.

choices.text: Class II — soft palate and uvula visible but pillars obscured; generally favorable
choices.rationale: Incorrect: Class II still permits visualization of the uvula in its entirety, whereas here only the uvular base is seen.

choices.text: Class III — only the soft palate and the base of the uvula are visible; associated with increased likelihood of difficult laryngoscopy
choices.rationale: Correct: visualization limited to the soft palate and uvular base defines class III, which correlates with a higher rate of difficult intubation.

choices.text: Class IV — only the hard palate is visible; predicts the most difficult view
choices.rationale: Incorrect: class IV is reserved for cases in which the soft palate cannot be seen at all and only the hard palate is visible.

choices.text: Class 0 — the epiglottis is visible; an extremely favorable view
choices.rationale: Incorrect: class 0 requires direct visualization of the epiglottis, which is not described here.

explanation: The modified Mallampati classification is performed with the patient seated, mouth maximally open, tongue protruded, and without phonation. Class I shows the soft palate, fauces, uvula, and pillars; class II shows the soft palate, fauces, and uvula; class III shows only the soft palate and the base of the uvula; and class IV shows only the hard palate. Higher classes (III and IV) are associated with an increased risk of difficult laryngoscopy, though the test has modest sensitivity and should be combined with other predictors such as thyromental distance and neck mobility.
subtopic: Preoperative airway assessment and difficult airway prediction

verdict.reason:
Answer is correct and unambiguous. The stem describes a view limited to the soft palate and the BASE of the uvula (tip and pillars obscured), which maps exactly to modified Mallampati Class III. Only one choice fits, and each distractor is genuinely inferior: Class I requires visible pillars (obscured here); Class II requires the ENTIRE uvula (only the base is seen here); Class IV is hard-palate-only; Class 0 requires a visible epiglottis. All rationales are accurate. The technique description (seated, mouth maximally open, tongue protruded, no phonation) correctly reflects the modified (Samsoon-Young) Mallampati. The explanation's class definitions and the modest-sensitivity caveat (test should be combined with other predictors) are medically accurate and current.

Reference check: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK585119/ resolves to the on-topic StatPearls "Mallampati Score" chapter, which gives the same class definitions verbatim (Class 0 epiglottis; I soft palate/uvula/pillars; II soft palate+uvula, pillars obscured; III soft palate+uvula base; IV hard palate only) and the ~0.51 sensitivity figure supporting the explanation. Reference is genuine and matches.

Only nitpick (not a defect): the thyromental distance of 5 cm and thyromegaly are deliberate distractor details that don't affect the Mallampati class determination, which is the intended teaching point.

stem: A 58-year-old man with longstanding poorly controlled hypertension (home BP often 170/100 mmHg) sustains a severe traumatic brain injury in a motor vehicle collision. An intraventricular catheter reads an intracranial pressure (ICP) of 22 mmHg. On arrival his blood pressure is 150/90 mmHg (MAP 110 mmHg). The neurosurgical team asks you to target a cerebral perfusion pressure (CPP) of 60-70 mmHg per Brain Trauma Foundation guidance while you manage his anesthetic for ICP control. Compared with a previously normotensive patient, which feature of this patient's cerebral autoregulation most directly increases his risk of cerebral ischemia if his MAP is allowed to fall toward 65 mmHg?

choices.text: Chronic hypertension shifts the autoregulatory curve to the right, raising the lower limit of autoregulation so ischemia occurs at a higher MAP
choices.rationale: Correct: sustained hypertension shifts the entire autoregulation curve rightward, elevating the lower autoregulatory threshold so that a MAP tolerated by a normotensive patient may already be below this patient's ischemic limit.

choices.text: Chronic hypertension shifts the autoregulatory curve to the left, lowering the threshold at which cerebral blood flow becomes pressure-passive
choices.rationale: Incorrect: chronic hypertension shifts the curve to the right, not left; a leftward shift would make him more tolerant of low MAP, the opposite of the actual risk.

choices.text: Chronic hypertension abolishes autoregulation entirely so cerebral blood flow is linearly pressure-dependent across all MAP values
choices.rationale: Incorrect: autoregulation is preserved but its operating range is shifted, not abolished, in chronic hypertension.

choices.text: Chronic hypertension widens the autoregulatory plateau, broadening the MAP range over which cerebral blood flow stays constant
choices.rationale: Incorrect: chronic hypertension does not widen the plateau; if anything the curve shifts rightward and the safe range relative to baseline narrows.

choices.text: Chronic hypertension lowers the upper limit of autoregulation, making hyperemia the dominant risk at a MAP of 65 mmHg
choices.rationale: Incorrect: the upper limit shifts upward (rightward) in chronic hypertension, and a MAP of 65 mmHg poses an ischemic—not hyperemic—threat.

explanation: Cerebral autoregulation maintains roughly constant cerebral blood flow across a MAP of about 60-160 mmHg (CPP ~50-150 mmHg) in healthy adults. Chronic hypertension shifts this curve to the right, raising both the lower and upper limits, so a chronically hypertensive patient becomes ischemic at a MAP that a normotensive patient would tolerate. This is why guidelines caution against aggressively lowering MAP and recommend keeping pressure within roughly 20-25% of baseline; in TBI, driving CPP to the low end of a generic target may fall below this patient's shifted lower autoregulatory limit.
subtopic: Cerebral autoregulation and CPP management in traumatic brain injury

verdict.reason: Sound question. The keyed answer (index 0) is the single best answer and is medically correct: chronic hypertension shifts the cerebral autoregulation curve rightward, raising the lower limit so ischemia occurs at a higher MAP. All four distractors are genuinely incorrect with no second defensible option — leftward shift (opposite), abolished autoregulation (preserved but shifted), widened plateau (does not occur), and lowered upper limit/hyperemia (both wrong; MAP 65 is an ischemic not hyperemic threat). The clinical scenario is internally consistent: ICP 22 + MAP 110 gives CPP 88 now, and letting MAP fall to ~65 would drop CPP to ~43, below the BTF 60-70 target and below this patient's right-shifted lower autoregulatory limit. The explanation's figures are accurate and current (MAP 60-160 / CPP ~50-150 at normal ICP; keep MAP within 20-25% of baseline). Reference (https://www.openanesthesia.org/keywords/cerebral-autoregulation/) resolves and is directly on-topic — it explicitly states 'With hypertension, the cerebral autoregulation curve shifts to the right' and gives the 20-25%-of-baseline guidance, matching the question. No clinically significant errors found.

stem: A 44-year-old woman undergoes resection of a posterior fossa meningioma in the sitting position under general anesthesia. About 90 minutes into the case, while the surgeon is working near a venous sinus, the end-tidal CO2 abruptly falls from 35 to 19 mmHg, the precordial Doppler emits a sudden harsh 'mill-wheel' churning sound, and the blood pressure drops to 78/44 mmHg. A right atrial catheter is in place. After asking the surgeon to flood the field with saline and applying jugular venous compression, which intervention is the most appropriate immediate next step?

choices.text: Place the patient in steep reverse Trendelenburg to lower venous pressure at the surgical site
choices.rationale: Incorrect: reverse Trendelenburg would worsen the situation by further lowering pressure at the wound, promoting continued air entrainment.

choices.text: Discontinue nitrous oxide if in use, deliver 100% oxygen, and aspirate air from the right atrial catheter
choices.rationale: Correct: stopping nitrous oxide prevents expansion of entrained air, 100% oxygen supports oxygenation and aids nitrogen washout from the bubble, and aspirating the right atrial catheter directly removes air from the heart.

choices.text: Increase the tidal volume and add 10 cmH2O of PEEP to raise mean airway pressure and tamponade the venous opening
choices.rationale: Incorrect: high PEEP is unreliable for stopping entrainment and can promote paradoxical air embolism via a patent foramen ovale by raising right atrial pressure.

choices.text: Administer a 500 mL crystalloid bolus and start a phenylephrine infusion before any other maneuver
choices.rationale: Incorrect: although hemodynamic support is appropriate, it is adjunctive and should not precede stopping nitrous oxide, giving 100% oxygen, and aspirating the catheter.

choices.text: Hyperventilate the patient to an end-tidal CO2 of 25 mmHg to reduce cerebral blood volume and brain bulk
choices.rationale: Incorrect: hyperventilation does not treat venous air embolism and the low end-tidal CO2 here reflects dead-space from the embolism, not a target to chase.

explanation: Venous air embolism is a major hazard of sitting-position posterior fossa surgery, where the wound lies above the heart and open venous channels entrain air. The classic signs are a sudden drop in end-tidal CO2 (increased dead space), a mill-wheel murmur or Doppler change, and hypotension. Management is to notify the surgeon to occlude the source (flood the field, wax bone edges, jugular compression), discontinue nitrous oxide and give 100% oxygen to prevent bubble expansion and aid resorption, aspirate air through a right atrial catheter, and support hemodynamics. Nitrous oxide is contraindicated once VAE is suspected because it rapidly enlarges the air bubble.
subtopic: Venous air embolism in sitting-position posterior fossa surgery

verdict.reason: Clinically sound question with a single defensible best answer. The key (B: discontinue N2O, give 100% O2, aspirate the right atrial catheter) bundles the three core time-critical VAE interventions and is unambiguously correct. All four distractors are genuinely inferior/incorrect with accurate rationales: reverse Trendelenburg (A) worsens entrainment (Durant/Trendelenburg is correct); high PEEP (C) is unreliable and raises RAP risking paradoxical embolism; fluid/phenylephrine "before any other maneuver" (D) is correctly framed as adjunctive; hyperventilation (E) does not treat VAE. The explanation is medically accurate and current. REFERENCE CHECK PASSED: WebFetch of https://www.openanesthesia.org/keywords/venous-air-embolism/ resolved to a page entirely focused on venous air embolism, confirming the signs (falling EtCO2, mill-wheel murmur, precordial Doppler) and management (100% O2 with N2O discontinuation, RA catheter aspiration, hemodynamic support) — genuinely on-topic, not a fabricated/mismatched link. MINOR issue only: the stem describes the precordial Doppler "emitting a mill-wheel churning sound." Strictly, the mill-wheel murmur is an auscultatory finding (esophageal/precordial stethoscope), whereas a precordial Doppler produces a roaring/swishing tonal change; the question conflates the two. This terminology imprecision does not create answer ambiguity and the keyed answer remains clearly best. Recommend rewording to either "the esophageal stethoscope reveals a mill-wheel murmur" or "the precordial Doppler tone changes to a harsh roaring/churning sound."

stem: A 16-year-old girl is undergoing posterior spinal instrumentation and fusion for adolescent idiopathic scoliosis. Intraoperative neuromonitoring includes both somatosensory evoked potentials (SSEPs) and transcranial motor evoked potentials (MEPs). The neurophysiologist tells you that the MEP signals have become difficult to obtain and asks you to optimize the anesthetic so that reliable MEP recordings are possible during the correction maneuver. Which anesthetic management change is most likely to improve the quality of the transcranial MEP recordings?

choices.text: Maintain a continuous nondepolarizing neuromuscular blockade titrated to a train-of-four of two twitches
choices.rationale: Incorrect: neuromuscular blockade attenuates the compound muscle action potential that MEPs depend on, degrading rather than improving motor recordings.

choices.text: Increase the inhaled sevoflurane concentration to 1.5 MAC to deepen anesthesia and reduce movement
choices.rationale: Incorrect: halogenated volatile agents depress MEP amplitude in a dose-dependent fashion (affecting MEPs more than SSEPs), so increasing sevoflurane worsens the signal.

choices.text: Transition to a total intravenous anesthetic with propofol and an opioid infusion while minimizing volatile agent
choices.rationale: Correct: propofol-based TIVA with an opioid suppresses MEPs far less than volatile agents, making it the preferred technique for reliable motor monitoring.

choices.text: Add a high-dose bolus of dexmedetomidine to provide background sedation and reduce volatile requirements
choices.rationale: Incorrect: while low-dose dexmedetomidine may be an adjunct, high-dose boluses can depress MEP amplitude and are not the primary fix for poor signals.

choices.text: Deepen the anesthetic with a bolus of nitrous oxide added to the volatile carrier gas
choices.rationale: Incorrect: nitrous oxide depresses evoked potential amplitudes, particularly MEPs, and adding it would further degrade the recordings.

explanation: Transcranial motor evoked potentials are highly sensitive to anesthetic depression, and halogenated volatile agents and nitrous oxide reduce MEP amplitude in a dose-dependent manner—more so than they affect SSEPs. A propofol-based total intravenous anesthetic with an opioid is therefore the standard technique when reliable MEP monitoring is required, as in scoliosis correction. Because MEPs are recorded as compound muscle action potentials, neuromuscular blockade must be avoided or kept minimal; deep blockade abolishes the motor response even though SSEPs are unaffected.
subtopic: Anesthetic effects on intraoperative motor evoked potential monitoring
reference_title: Evoked Potentials: Modalities and Anesthetic Effects - OpenAnesthesia

verdict.reason:
Reference verified: WebFetch of the OpenAnesthesia "Evoked Potentials: Modalities and Anesthetic Effects" page resolves and is squarely on-topic, confirming every teaching point (volatiles and N2O depress MEPs more than SSEPs in a dose-dependent fashion; intravenous agents have lesser effect; low-dose NMB infusion reduces motor monitoring accuracy and is not routine).

Answer key is correct and unambiguous: switching to propofol-based TIVA with an opioid while minimizing volatile is the standard technique when reliable MEP monitoring is needed (scoliosis correction). This is the single best answer.

Distractors are all genuinely inferior/incorrect: (A) continuous NMB to TOF of 2 attenuates the CMAP that myogenic MEPs depend on; (B) increasing sevoflurane to 1.5 MAC further depresses MEP amplitude; (D) high-dose dexmedetomidine bolus can depress MEPs and does not address the volatile-based problem (rationale correctly preserves the nuance that low-dose dex is a tolerated adjunct); (E) adding N2O further depresses MEP amplitude. No two-defensible-answer issue.

Rationales and explanation are medically accurate and current, including the correct points that MEPs are recorded as compound muscle action potentials and that SSEPs are spared by NMB while MEPs are abolished. No clinically significant inaccuracies. Question is sound.

stem: A 33-year-old G1 woman at 35 weeks with preeclampsia with severe features is receiving an intravenous magnesium sulfate infusion for seizure prophylaxis. Her blood pressure is controlled at 148/94 mmHg. Over the past 30 minutes she has become increasingly drowsy and reports flushing and weakness. On examination her patellar deep tendon reflexes are absent, respiratory rate is 10/min, and oxygen saturation is 93% on room air. Urine output has fallen to 10 mL/hr. What is the most appropriate immediate intervention?

choices.text: Increase the magnesium sulfate infusion rate to better control her neurologic symptoms
choices.rationale: Her drowsiness, areflexia, and respiratory depression are signs of magnesium toxicity, so increasing the dose would deepen the toxicity and risk respiratory and cardiac arrest.

choices.text: Administer a benzodiazepine for presumed impending eclamptic seizure
choices.rationale: Her depressed sensorium reflects magnesium toxicity rather than seizure activity, and a benzodiazepine would further depress respiration.

choices.text: Administer furosemide to increase urine output and accelerate magnesium clearance
choices.rationale: Diuresis is too slow to reverse acute respiratory depression and risks worsening hypovolemia, so it does not address the immediate threat.

choices.text: Stop the magnesium infusion and administer intravenous calcium gluconate
choices.rationale: Stopping the magnesium and giving IV calcium (the direct physiologic antagonist) is the correct management of acute magnesium toxicity with respiratory depression and areflexia.

choices.text: Administer naloxone to reverse the respiratory depression
choices.rationale: Naloxone reverses opioid-induced respiratory depression but has no effect on magnesium-induced neuromuscular and respiratory depression.

explanation: Loss of deep tendon reflexes is the earliest reliable clinical sign of magnesium toxicity (typically around 8-10 mg/dL), followed by respiratory depression (~12 mg/dL) and ultimately cardiac arrest at higher levels. Oliguria from preeclampsia reduces renal magnesium clearance and predisposes to accumulation. Immediate management is to stop the infusion and give IV calcium gluconate (1 g) or calcium chloride, which directly antagonizes magnesium at the neuromuscular junction, while supporting ventilation. Reflexes should be monitored at the bedside during any magnesium infusion.
subtopic: Hypertensive disorders of pregnancy — magnesium sulfate toxicity

verdict.reason: Sound, high-quality question. The keyed answer (D: stop magnesium + IV calcium gluconate) is the single, unambiguous best answer for acute magnesium sulfate toxicity with areflexia and respiratory depression — IV calcium is the direct physiologic antagonist at the NMJ, and stopping the offending infusion is mandatory. All four distractors are genuinely wrong/inferior: increasing Mg (A) deepens toxicity, benzodiazepine (B) misreads toxicity as seizure and worsens respiratory depression, furosemide (C) is too slow for acute respiratory compromise, and naloxone (E) has no effect on Mg-induced depression. The clinical vignette is internally consistent and textbook (drowsiness, flushing, weakness, absent patellar DTRs, RR 10, oliguria reducing renal Mg clearance). Rationales and explanation are medically accurate and current: loss of DTRs as the earliest reliable sign (~8-10 mg/dL), respiratory depression (~12 mg/dL), cardiac arrest at higher levels, renal clearance dependence, and the 1 g IV calcium gluconate dose are all correct. Reference check: WebFetch of NBK430839 resolves to the real, on-topic StatPearls chapter "Hypertension In Pregnancy," which covers preeclampsia and magnesium seizure prophylaxis. It does not detail toxicity thresholds/calcium antidote specifically, but it is genuinely on-topic, real, and not fabricated/irrelevant — adequate for the question's subtopic.

stem: A 38-year-old G4P3 woman with chronic hypertension delivers a 4,300 g infant after a prolonged second stage. Her current blood pressure is 168/102 mmHg. Within minutes of delivery she has brisk vaginal bleeding; the uterus is boggy and palpable above the umbilicus. Oxytocin is already infusing at a high rate and fundal massage has been started, but bleeding continues and the uterus remains atonic. Which second-line uterotonic is most appropriate to administer next?

choices.text: Carboprost (15-methyl PGF2-alpha) 250 mcg intramuscularly
choices.rationale: Carboprost effectively treats atony and is the preferred second-line agent here because the contraindicated alternative is the ergot, though it should be used cautiously given any reactive airway disease.

choices.text: Methylergonovine 200 mcg intramuscularly
choices.rationale: Methylergonovine is contraindicated in this hypertensive patient because its alpha-adrenergic vasoconstriction can precipitate a severe, sometimes fatal, hypertensive crisis or stroke.

choices.text: A repeat intravenous bolus of oxytocin 40 units given as rapid push
choices.rationale: Rapid undiluted oxytocin boluses cause vasodilation, hypotension, and tachycardia, and simply escalating an agent that has already failed does not add a new mechanism.

choices.text: Intravenous magnesium sulfate to relax the uterus and reduce blood pressure
choices.rationale: Magnesium is a tocolytic that relaxes uterine smooth muscle and would worsen atony and bleeding, the opposite of what is needed.

choices.text: Terbutaline 250 mcg subcutaneously
choices.rationale: Terbutaline is a beta-agonist tocolytic that further relaxes the uterus, aggravating atony and hemorrhage.

explanation: This is uterine atony, the leading cause of primary postpartum hemorrhage, with classic risk factors of grand multiparity, macrosomia, and prolonged labor. When oxytocin and massage fail, a second-line uterotonic with a different mechanism is indicated. Methylergonovine (an ergot alkaloid) is contraindicated in hypertension and preeclampsia because of its vasoconstrictive effect, so carboprost is the appropriate choice here — with the caveat that prostaglandin F2-alpha analogues can cause bronchospasm and should be avoided in significant asthma. Misoprostol is another option when both ergots and prostaglandin injectables are contraindicated.
subtopic: Obstetric hemorrhage — uterotonic selection in uterine atony

verdict.reason:
Verified against the reference (NBK499988 "Postpartum Hemorrhage," StatPearls) via WebFetch — it resolves and is directly on-topic, explicitly stating methylergonovine is relatively contraindicated in hypertension and carboprost may cause bronchospasm in asthma.

Answer key is correct and unambiguous. This is classic uterine atony (grand multiparity, macrosomia at 4,300 g, prolonged second stage) refractory to oxytocin + massage. With chronic hypertension and an acutely elevated BP of 168/102, methylergonovine (ergot, alpha-adrenergic vasoconstrictor) is contraindicated, so carboprost (15-methyl PGF2-alpha) 250 mcg IM is the appropriate next agent. Doses are correct: carboprost 250 mcg IM and methylergonovine 200 mcg (0.2 mg) are standard.

All distractors are genuinely inferior/incorrect:
- Methylergonovine (B): correctly contraindicated in this hypertensive patient — risk of hypertensive crisis/stroke.
- Oxytocin 40 U rapid IV push (C): rapid undiluted bolus causes hypotension, tachycardia, vasodilation, and adds no new mechanism after oxytocin already failed.
- Magnesium (D): tocolytic that relaxes uterine smooth muscle — would worsen atony.
- Terbutaline (E): beta-agonist tocolytic — also worsens atony.

No second defensible answer. The carboprost rationale's asthma caveat is appropriate general context (no asthma is given in the stem, so carboprost remains appropriate here). Rationales and explanation are medically accurate and current; misoprostol correctly noted as the fallback when both ergots and injectable prostaglandins are contraindicated. Only nitpick is slightly awkward phrasing in the carboprost rationale, which does not affect correctness.

stem: A 54-year-old man with a BMI of 41 kg/m2, hypertension, and type 2 diabetes is scheduled for an elective unilateral inguinal hernia repair at a freestanding ambulatory surgery center. He reports loud snoring, witnessed apneas, and daytime somnolence; his STOP-Bang score is 6. He has never had a formal sleep study and is not on CPAP. His blood pressure is 148/88 mmHg and SpO2 is 96% on room air. The surgeon plans general anesthesia with an LMA and expects to use intraoperative and postoperative opioids for analgesia. Which factor most strongly supports rescheduling this case at a facility with extended postoperative monitoring rather than proceeding at the freestanding center?

choices.text: The combination of a high STOP-Bang score (presumed severe OSA) with an anesthetic plan requiring substantial postoperative opioids
choices.rationale: Correct, because presumed-severe untreated OSA combined with an opioid-dependent analgesic plan markedly raises the risk of postoperative respiratory depression and airway obstruction, which exceeds what a freestanding center can safely monitor.

choices.text: His BMI of 41 kg/m2 alone, which is an absolute contraindication to ambulatory surgery
choices.rationale: Incorrect, because obesity by itself is not an absolute contraindication to ambulatory surgery; appropriately selected patients with high BMI can undergo outpatient procedures when comorbidities are optimized.

choices.text: His blood pressure of 148/88 mmHg, which mandates inpatient management
choices.rationale: Incorrect, because stage 1 hypertension without evidence of end-organ ischemia does not require inpatient management and is not a reason to cancel or transfer an ambulatory case.

choices.text: The planned use of an LMA, which is unsafe in patients with obstructive sleep apnea
choices.rationale: Incorrect, because a supraglottic airway is not contraindicated in OSA and the airway device choice is not the principal driver of postoperative disposition risk.

choices.text: His type 2 diabetes, which precludes same-day discharge after general anesthesia
choices.rationale: Incorrect, because well-controlled diabetes is routinely managed in the ambulatory setting and does not by itself preclude same-day discharge.

explanation: Patient selection for the freestanding ambulatory setting hinges on matching procedural and anesthetic risk to the facility's monitoring capability. A patient with presumed severe, untreated OSA (STOP-Bang 6) who will require significant postoperative opioids faces a meaningfully elevated risk of opioid-induced respiratory depression and airway obstruction; the SAMBA/ASA guidance favors an opioid-sparing, multimodal plan and a facility capable of extended monitoring when severe OSA cannot be mitigated. Isolated factors such as obesity, controlled hypertension, or diabetes are not independent disqualifiers.
subtopic: Patient selection and facility appropriateness (obstructive sleep apnea)

verdict.reason:
Keyed answer (A) is the single best answer and is clinically sound: the discriminating risk driver for facility selection in this scenario is the interaction of presumed-severe untreated OSA (STOP-Bang 6) with an opioid-heavy postoperative analgesic plan. This matches SAMBA consensus and ASA OSA guidance — patients with presumed OSA can sometimes proceed at an ambulatory center if pain is controlled with opioid-sparing/multimodal techniques; the inability to do so is what pushes toward extended monitoring. Each distractor is genuinely inferior because it relies on a false absolutist claim: BMI 41 is not an absolute contraindication (B); stage HTN at 148/88 does not mandate inpatient care (C); well-controlled T2DM does not preclude same-day discharge (D); an LMA/supraglottic airway is not contraindicated in OSA and is not the principal disposition driver (E). No second defensible answer; logic of single-best-answer holds.

Reference check: WebFetch of https://www.openanesthesia.org/keywords/obstructive-sleep-apnea/ resolves successfully and is genuinely on-topic — it is an OpenAnesthesia page on OSA pathophysiology that explicitly addresses opioids reducing respiratory drive and impairing arousal, i.e., the core mechanism (opioid-induced respiratory depression in OSA) underlying the keyed answer. Not fabricated, not off-topic. However it is a pathophysiology page that does not directly cover ambulatory patient-selection criteria or STOP-Bang, so it only partially supports the explanation's specific 'SAMBA/ASA guidance' claim — a suboptimal but acceptable reference match.

Minor issues only: (1) the option-C rationale labels 148/88 mmHg as 'stage 1 hypertension'; by current ACC/AHA 2017 criteria a systolic of 148 is stage 2 — a classification imprecision that does not change the conclusion (it still does not mandate inpatient management) or affect the keyed answer. (2) The reference, while on-topic, would be stronger if it pointed to the SAMBA OSA ambulatory consensus statement. Neither issue rises to major; the question is medically accurate and well-constructed.

stem: A healthy 24-year-old nonsmoking woman is scheduled for an outpatient laparoscopic cholecystectomy under general anesthesia. She reports severe nausea and vomiting after a prior tonsillectomy and a history of motion sickness. The planned anesthetic includes volatile agent maintenance and intraoperative opioids. Recognizing she has four Apfel risk factors, the anesthesia team wants to minimize her postoperative nausea and vomiting (PONV) risk to avoid an unplanned admission. Which intervention is the most effective single modification to reduce her baseline PONV risk?

choices.text: Administering a single prophylactic dose of ondansetron at the end of surgery as the only intervention
choices.rationale: Incorrect, because a single antiemetic provides only modest relative risk reduction and is inadequate as sole prophylaxis in a patient with four risk factors.

choices.text: Replacing volatile-based maintenance with propofol total intravenous anesthesia (TIVA) and minimizing opioids
choices.rationale: Correct, because substituting a propofol-based TIVA for volatile maintenance and reducing opioids directly removes two major anesthetic triggers, lowering the patient's baseline risk before pharmacologic prophylaxis is added.

choices.text: Adding nitrous oxide to reduce the required volatile concentration
choices.rationale: Incorrect, because nitrous oxide is itself an independent emetogenic agent and would increase rather than decrease PONV risk.

choices.text: Withholding all antiemetic prophylaxis and treating nausea only if it occurs in recovery
choices.rationale: Incorrect, because a rescue-only strategy is inappropriate for a high-risk patient and ignores the value of reducing baseline risk and combining prophylactic agents.

choices.text: Giving generous intraoperative opioids to ensure deep analgesia and prevent pain-related nausea
choices.rationale: Incorrect, because opioids are a dose-dependent cause of PONV, so liberal opioid use raises rather than lowers her risk.

explanation: In a patient with four Apfel risk factors, the most impactful step is to lower baseline risk by modifying the anesthetic itself rather than relying on a single antiemetic. Switching from volatile maintenance to propofol TIVA and minimizing opioids each independently reduce PONV, and these baseline reductions are then layered with multimodal pharmacologic prophylaxis using agents from different receptor classes. Nitrous oxide and liberal opioids both worsen PONV and should be avoided in this setting.
subtopic: Postoperative nausea and vomiting prophylaxis and risk stratification
reference_title: Postoperative Nausea and Vomiting in Adults (OpenAnesthesia)

verdict.reason: Keyed answer (B: propofol TIVA replacing volatile maintenance + minimizing opioids) is the single best answer and is unambiguous. The stem specifically asks for the most effective modification to reduce BASELINE PONV risk, which per the 4th consensus/Apfel-SAMBA framework is precisely anesthetic-technique modification (TIVA, avoiding volatiles, minimizing opioids) — distinct from layered antiemetic prophylaxis. The four Apfel risk factors map correctly (female, nonsmoker, prior PONV/motion sickness, planned postop opioids). All distractors are genuinely inferior and their rationales are accurate: (A) single antiemetic gives only modest RRR and is inadequate for a 4-factor patient; (C) N2O is independently emetogenic; (D) rescue-only is inappropriate for high-risk; (E) opioids are dose-dependently emetogenic. The explanation is medically accurate and current. REFERENCE: WebFetch of the OpenAnesthesia PONV keywords URL resolves and is directly on-topic — it covers Apfel stratification, propofol TIVA over volatiles, minimizing N2O/opioids, and multimodal prophylaxis with agents of differing mechanisms. Only nitpick (does not affect correctness): choice B technically bundles two actions while the stem says 'single modification,' but the dominant single technique change (TIVA replacing volatile) is clear and creates no ambiguity.

stem: During an elective outpatient knee arthroscopy under general anesthesia with sevoflurane and a single dose of succinylcholine for intubation at a freestanding surgery center, a 19-year-old man develops an unexplained rise in end-tidal CO2 to 68 mmHg despite increased minute ventilation, masseter rigidity, tachycardia to 140 bpm, and a temperature rising from 36.8 to 39.1 C over 20 minutes. Malignant hyperthermia is suspected. After calling for help and discontinuing the triggering agents, what is the single most important next pharmacologic intervention?

choices.text: Administer intravenous dantrolene 2.5 mg/kg immediately and repeat as needed
choices.rationale: Correct, because dantrolene is the only definitive treatment for an MH crisis and must be given without delay at an initial dose of 2.5 mg/kg, titrated until the hypermetabolic signs resolve.

choices.text: Administer a beta-blocker to control the tachycardia
choices.rationale: Incorrect, because the tachycardia reflects the underlying hypermetabolic crisis, and beta-blockade neither treats the cause nor addresses the life-threatening hyperthermia and acidosis.

choices.text: Give intravenous sodium bicarbonate as the primary treatment for the crisis
choices.rationale: Incorrect, because while bicarbonate may be used to help correct metabolic acidosis, it is an adjunct and does not reverse the underlying ryanodine-receptor calcium dysregulation.

choices.text: Administer a calcium channel blocker such as verapamil to lower intracellular calcium
choices.rationale: Incorrect, because calcium channel blockers do not treat MH and can cause dangerous hyperkalemia and cardiovascular collapse when combined with dantrolene.

choices.text: Deepen anesthesia with additional sevoflurane to control the sympathetic response
choices.rationale: Incorrect, because sevoflurane is itself an MH trigger and continuing it would worsen the crisis rather than control it.

explanation: Malignant hyperthermia is a pharmacogenetic hypermetabolic crisis triggered by volatile agents and succinylcholine; the earliest and most sensitive sign is an unexplained rise in end-tidal CO2, often with masseter rigidity after succinylcholine. After stopping triggers, calling for help, and hyperventilating with 100% oxygen, dantrolene 2.5 mg/kg IV is the definitive treatment and must be given immediately and repeated until the hypermetabolic state resolves. Because MH can occur in any setting that uses triggering agents, every ambulatory facility administering volatile anesthetics or succinylcholine must stock a full supply of dantrolene and a transfer plan.
subtopic: Perioperative emergencies in the ambulatory setting (malignant hyperthermia)

verdict.reason: Adversarial review passed. (1) Answer logic: The stem is a textbook MH crisis (rising EtCO2 refractory to ventilation, masseter rigidity post-succinylcholine, tachycardia, rapid temperature rise after sevoflurane + succinylcholine). The keyed answer A (dantrolene 2.5 mg/kg IV immediately, repeat as needed) is the single best answer; the qualifier "single most important next PHARMACOLOGIC intervention" cleanly excludes non-drug measures (cooling, stopping triggers, 100% O2), leaving dantrolene with no defensible competitor. Each distractor is genuinely inferior/incorrect: beta-blocker (treats symptom not cause), bicarbonate (adjunct for acidosis only), verapamil/CCB (does not treat MH and the dantrolene+verapamil interaction causing hyperkalemia/cardiovascular collapse is well-documented — accurate), and additional sevoflurane (the trigger itself). (2) Rationales and explanation are medically accurate and current: dantrolene initial dose 2.5 mg/kg titrated to resolution of hypermetabolic signs, EtCO2 rise as earliest/most sensitive sign, MH pathophysiology (ryanodine-receptor calcium dysregulation), and the ambulatory-specific point that any facility using triggers must stock full dantrolene supply and have a transfer plan. (3) Reference: WebFetch of https://www.openanesthesia.org/keywords/malignant-hyperthermia/ resolved and is genuinely on-topic — it covers MH triggers, signs, and states verbatim "The initial dose of dantrolene is 2.5mg/kg. Repeat as frequently as needed... The average effective dose is 5 mg/kg," directly supporting the keyed answer.

stem: A 72-year-old man with benign prostatic hyperplasia undergoes monopolar transurethral resection of the prostate (TURP) under spinal anesthesia with 1.5% glycine irrigation. About 50 minutes into the resection, he becomes restless and reports nausea and blurred vision, then complains of a headache. His blood pressure rises to 168/96 mmHg, heart rate falls to 52/min, and SpO2 is 97% on supplemental oxygen. A stat serum sodium returns at 118 mEq/L. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

choices.text: Ask the surgeon to stop the resection and irrigation, then begin 3% hypertonic saline at approximately 1 mL/kg/hr
choices.rationale: Correct: this patient has symptomatic, severe TURP-syndrome hyponatremia, so the irrigant absorption source must be halted and slow correction with hypertonic saline initiated while limiting the rise to about 10 mEq/L per 24 hours.

choices.text: Rapidly infuse 1 liter of 0.9% normal saline to restore the sodium toward normal
choices.rationale: Incorrect: isotonic saline corrects sodium far too slowly for symptomatic severe hyponatremia and adds volume to an already fluid-overloaded patient.

choices.text: Administer atropine and continue the resection while monitoring the sodium
choices.rationale: Incorrect: treating only the bradycardia while continuing irrigation allows further hypotonic fluid absorption and progression toward seizures, coma, and death.

choices.text: Give 0.5 mEq/kg of sodium bicarbonate to correct the presumed acidosis
choices.rationale: Incorrect: the problem is dilutional hyponatremia and cerebral edema, not acidosis, and bicarbonate does not address the sodium deficit or the ongoing absorption.

choices.text: Restrict all intravenous and irrigation fluids and observe without sodium therapy
choices.rationale: Incorrect: passive fluid restriction is inadequate for a symptomatic patient at 118 mEq/L who needs active, controlled sodium correction to prevent cerebral herniation.

explanation: TURP syndrome results from intravascular absorption of hypotonic irrigation fluid through open prostatic venous sinuses, producing dilutional hyponatremia, volume overload, and cerebral edema; glycine solutions add hyperammonemia and transient visual disturbances. The first step is always to stop the irrigation/resection source, secure oxygenation, and obtain a stat sodium. Symptomatic severe hyponatremia (e.g., 118 mEq/L with neurologic signs) warrants 3% saline at about 1 mL/kg/hr with the correction capped near 10 mEq/L per 24 hours to avoid osmotic demyelination.
subtopic: Urology: TURP syndrome (dilutional hyponatremia from irrigation fluid absorption)
reference_title: Hyponatremia and Transurethral Resection of Prostate Syndrome - OpenAnesthesia

verdict.reason:
Reference verified: the OpenAnesthesia URL resolves and is squarely on-topic (TURP syndrome, dilutional hyponatremia from hypotonic irrigant absorption, glycine-related hyperammonemia/visual disturbances, and management with 3% saline capped at ~10 mEq/L/24h to avoid osmotic demyelination). It directly supports the keyed answer, including the "1 mL/kg/hr" figure.

Answer is correct and unambiguous. Option A is the single best choice: in symptomatic severe TURP-syndrome hyponatremia (Na 118 with restlessness, nausea, blurred vision, headache, plus a Cushing-like HTN/bradycardia from cerebral edema), the priority is to halt the absorption source (stop irrigation/resection) and begin controlled 3% hypertonic saline with slow correction. Each distractor is genuinely inferior: (B) isotonic saline corrects too slowly and worsens volume overload; (C) atropine + continued resection perpetuates hypotonic absorption and risks seizure/coma; (D) bicarbonate addresses a non-existent acidosis; (E) passive fluid restriction is inadequate for a symptomatic patient at 118.

Adversarial check on a possible second-best answer: severe-symptom guidelines sometimes favor a 100 mL 3% saline bolus, but no bolus option is offered, the patient lacks seizure/coma, and a 1 mL/kg/hr infusion is appropriate and matches the cited source — so there is no competing defensible key. Rationales and explanation are medically accurate and current. Sound question.

stem: A 24-year-old healthy man undergoes open reduction and internal fixation of a tibial plateau fracture under general anesthesia with a thigh pneumatic tourniquet inflated for 95 minutes. He is volume-replete and normothermic, ventilated to an end-tidal CO2 of 35 mmHg. Immediately after the surgeon deflates the tourniquet, the anesthetist notes the end-tidal CO2 climbs to 48 mmHg over about one minute and the mean arterial pressure falls from 78 to 60 mmHg. Which of the following best explains these findings?

choices.text: Acute pulmonary embolism from dislodged deep venous thrombus
choices.rationale: Incorrect: a large PE would classically cause a fall (not a rise) in end-tidal CO2 from increased dead-space ventilation, along with hypoxemia and right-heart strain.

choices.text: Washout of CO2-rich, vasoactive metabolites from the previously ischemic limb into the central circulation
choices.rationale: Correct: reperfusion of the ischemic limb returns accumulated CO2, lactate, and potassium centrally, transiently raising end-tidal CO2 and lowering systemic vascular resistance and arterial pressure.

choices.text: Malignant hyperthermia triggered by the volatile anesthetic
choices.rationale: Incorrect: MH produces a sustained, progressive rise in end-tidal CO2 with hyperthermia, rigidity, and tachycardia rather than a brief spike timed precisely to cuff release.

choices.text: Inadequate minute ventilation from a kinked or disconnected circuit
choices.rationale: Incorrect: a circuit problem is unrelated to tourniquet deflation timing and would not resolve spontaneously as reperfusion metabolites are cleared over several minutes.

choices.text: Light anesthesia causing sympathetic activation and hypercarbia
choices.rationale: Incorrect: light anesthesia typically raises blood pressure and heart rate, whereas this patient shows hypotension coincident with cuff deflation, which is the opposite hemodynamic picture.

explanation: Release of a lower-extremity pneumatic tourniquet allows CO2-rich, acidotic, hyperkalemic venous blood from the ischemic limb to re-enter the systemic circulation, producing a transient spike in end-tidal CO2 (peaking around one minute and normalizing within about ten minutes) and a fall in central venous pressure and arterial pressure that can persist roughly fifteen minutes. The metabolic washout also causes a brief metabolic acidosis. Anticipatory management includes transiently increasing minute ventilation and ensuring euvolemia before deflation. The hemodynamic dip is usually self-limited but can be marked in hypovolemic patients.
subtopic: Orthopedics: pneumatic tourniquet deflation physiology
reference_title: Lower extremity tourniquet deflation: Physiologic effects - OpenAnesthesia

verdict.reason: Keyed answer (washout of CO2-rich/acidotic/hyperkalemic metabolites from the reperfused limb) is the single best answer. The clinical signature is pathognomonic and cleanly excludes every distractor: the EtCO2 RISES immediately on deflation (peaking ~1 min) rather than falling as it would with PE dead-space; the patient is normothermic with a brief deflation-timed spike, excluding MH (sustained, progressive, hyperthermic); a circuit problem is unrelated to cuff-release timing and wouldn't self-resolve or cause hypotension; and light anesthesia would RAISE BP/HR, the opposite of the observed hypotension. All four distractor rationales are medically accurate, as is the explanation (transient EtCO2 spike normalizing ~10 min, ~15-min fall in CVP/arterial pressure, metabolic acidosis, management with increased minute ventilation and euvolemia). REFERENCE VERIFIED: the OpenAnesthesia URL resolves and is directly on-topic; its content matches the explanation almost verbatim (EtCO2 peaks at 1 min, returns to baseline in 10 min; 15-min reduction in CVP/arterial BP; K+/lactate/acidosis washout). No ambiguity, no inaccuracy, no fabricated reference.

stem: A 38-year-old woman with no significant comorbidities undergoes laparoscopic hysterectomy under general anesthesia. Shortly after the surgeon insufflates the abdomen with CO2 to establish pneumoperitoneum, the patient develops sinus bradycardia to 38/min with a brief run of junctional rhythm; blood pressure falls to 84/50 mmHg. SpO2 remains 99% and end-tidal CO2 is stable at 36 mmHg. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this acute event?

choices.text: Carbon dioxide venous gas embolism
choices.rationale: Incorrect: a significant CO2 embolism typically causes a sudden fall in end-tidal CO2 with hypoxemia and cardiovascular collapse, whereas this patient's end-tidal CO2 and saturation are unchanged.

choices.text: Hypercarbia-induced catecholamine surge
choices.rationale: Incorrect: systemic CO2 absorption raises sympathetic tone causing tachycardia and hypertension, the opposite of the bradycardia and hypotension observed.

choices.text: A vagally mediated reflex from acute peritoneal stretch during insufflation
choices.rationale: Correct: rapid distension of the peritoneum at the onset of insufflation can trigger a vagal reflex producing bradyarrhythmias and hypotension, which is the classic timing and presentation here.

choices.text: Tension capnothorax from CO2 tracking into the pleural space
choices.rationale: Incorrect: a capnothorax produces rising airway pressures, hypoxemia, and hemodynamic compromise over time rather than an immediate vagal bradycardia at the moment of insufflation.

choices.text: Endobronchial migration of the endotracheal tube from cephalad diaphragm shift
choices.rationale: Incorrect: mainstem intubation causes hypoxemia and high peak airway pressures, not an abrupt isolated bradyarrhythmia, and the saturation here is normal.

explanation: At the onset of CO2 pneumoperitoneum, sudden peritoneal stretch can provoke a vagal reflex producing bradycardia, junctional rhythms, or even asystole, particularly with rapid insufflation. Appropriate management is to ask the surgeon to desufflate, treat with an anticholinergic such as atropine, and re-insufflate slowly at a lower pressure. This is distinct from the later sympathetic effects of absorbed CO2 (hypertension, tachycardia) and from CO2 venous embolism, which lowers end-tidal CO2 and oxygen saturation.
subtopic: GYN/laparoscopy: vagal bradyarrhythmia from CO2 pneumoperitoneum insufflation
reference_title: Anesthesia for Laparoscopic and Robotic Surgery - OpenAnesthesia

verdict.reason: Keyed answer (C, vagal reflex from acute peritoneal stretch) is the single best answer and is strongly supported by the literature: a pronounced vagal response with bradycardia/junctional rhythm/hypotension at the onset of CO2 insufflation occurs even in healthy patients, with preserved SpO2/EtCO2. All four distractors are genuinely inferior and correctly excluded by the vignette data: (A) CO2 venous embolism would LOWER EtCO2 and cause hypoxemia/collapse, but EtCO2 is stable at 36 and SpO2 99%; (B) hypercarbia catecholamine surge causes tachycardia/hypertension (opposite findings) and EtCO2 is normal anyway; (D) tension capnothorax evolves over time with rising airway pressures and hypoxemia, not an instantaneous bradyarrhythmia; (E) endobronchial intubation causes hypoxemia and high peak pressures, but SpO2 is 99%. Rationales and the explanation (including management: desufflate, atropine, slow low-pressure re-insufflation) are medically accurate and current. The reference_url (OpenAnesthesia, Anesthesia for Laparoscopic and Robotic Surgery) resolves and is directly on-topic, explicitly stating peritoneal stretch from insufflation can cause a vagal response and bradyarrhythmias/cardiac arrest. No errors found.

stem: A 68-kg woman undergoes an ultrasound-guided interscalene brachial plexus block with 30 mL of 0.5% ropivacaine for shoulder arthroscopy. About 90 seconds after injection she reports a metallic taste and circumoral numbness, then becomes agitated and has a generalized tonic-clonic seizure, followed by a wide-complex bradycardia with a blood pressure of 70/40 mmHg. The seizure is terminated with midazolam and ventilation is supported with 100% oxygen. In addition to starting chest compressions if a pulse is lost, which intervention is most appropriate as part of her resuscitation?

choices.text: Administer a standard ACLS epinephrine dose of 1 mg IV every 3 to 5 minutes
choices.rationale: During local anesthetic systemic toxicity, full ACLS-dose epinephrine can impair lipid resuscitation and worsen arrhythmias, so the dose should be reduced rather than given at the standard 1 mg.

choices.text: Give a calcium channel blocker to control the wide-complex rhythm
choices.rationale: Calcium channel blockers and beta-blockers are specifically discouraged in LAST because they further depress an already compromised myocardium.

choices.text: Administer a propofol infusion to both treat the seizure and provide a lipid load
choices.rationale: Propofol is not a substitute for lipid emulsion; its lipid content is far too low to be therapeutic and its cardiodepressant effect is dangerous in a hypotensive patient.

choices.text: Administer a 1.5 mL/kg bolus of 20% intravenous lipid emulsion followed by an infusion
choices.rationale: Intravenous lipid emulsion is the established standard treatment for LAST, given as an approximately 1.5 mL/kg bolus of the 20% formulation followed by a continuous infusion, and it should be started early once toxicity is recognized.

choices.text: Give vasopressin 40 units IV in place of epinephrine
choices.rationale: Vasopressin is not recommended in LAST resuscitation and has been associated with worse outcomes in this setting, so it should be avoided rather than substituted for epinephrine.

explanation: This is classic local anesthetic systemic toxicity (LAST): prodromal CNS symptoms (metallic taste, circumoral numbness), a seizure, and cardiovascular collapse shortly after a large-volume plexus injection. The cornerstone of treatment, alongside airway control, seizure suppression with benzodiazepines, and modified ACLS, is intravenous 20% lipid emulsion given as a ~1.5 mL/kg bolus followed by an infusion. During LAST, epinephrine doses should be reduced (to roughly 1 mcg/kg or less), and vasopressin, calcium channel blockers, beta-blockers, and additional local anesthetics should be avoided.
subtopic: Local anesthetic systemic toxicity (LAST) management
reference_title: Local Anesthetic Toxicity - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf

verdict.reason: Clinically sound LAST question with an unambiguous best answer. The stem is a classic LAST presentation: CNS prodrome (metallic taste, circumoral numbness), generalized seizure, then cardiovascular collapse (wide-complex bradycardia, hypotension) after a large-volume interscalene injection of ~150 mg ropivacaine. Keyed answer (index 3, IV 20% lipid emulsion 1.5 mL/kg bolus + infusion) is the single best answer and matches ASRA 2020 dosing; at 68 kg the patient falls under the <70 kg weight-based bolus (1.5 mL/kg), so this phrasing is exactly correct. All four distractors are genuinely inferior/incorrect and align with ASRA guidance: standard 1 mg ACLS epinephrine is wrong (dose should be reduced to ~1 mcg/kg), calcium channel blockers are explicitly discouraged, propofol is not a valid lipid substitute and is dangerously cardiodepressant in a hypotensive patient, and vasopressin should be avoided. Rationales and explanation are medically accurate and current. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK499964/ resolves to the StatPearls 'Local Anesthetic Toxicity' chapter — genuinely on-topic, confirming the CNS prodrome/seizure presentation, lipid emulsion 1.5 mL/kg bolus dosing for <70 kg, and epinephrine reduction to 1 mcg/kg. No errors found; no defensible second answer.

stem: A 58-year-old man undergoes an open right hemicolectomy. As part of a multimodal opioid-sparing plan, the surgical team performs bilateral ultrasound-guided transversus abdominis plane (TAP) blocks with local anesthetic. In the recovery unit he reports good relief of pain at his midline and flank incision sites but continues to have deep, cramping pain that he localizes vaguely to the center of his abdomen, with associated nausea. Which statement best explains why the TAP block has not relieved this component of his pain?

choices.text: The TAP block was placed too superficially and deposited local anesthetic in the subcutaneous tissue
choices.rationale: A technical failure would also reduce relief of his incisional (somatic) pain, but his wound pain is well controlled, so a misplaced injection does not explain the isolated deep abdominal pain.

choices.text: The TAP block anesthetizes the thoracolumbar somatic nerves of the abdominal wall but does not block visceral afferents
choices.rationale: The TAP block deposits local anesthetic around the T6–L1 somatic nerves supplying the anterior and lateral abdominal wall and parietal peritoneum, so it relieves incisional somatic pain but does not cover visceral afferent pain from the bowel.

choices.text: The TAP block only provides analgesia when combined with intrathecal morphine
choices.rationale: A TAP block produces analgesia on its own from local anesthetic spread to abdominal wall nerves and does not require intrathecal morphine to be effective.

choices.text: Cramping abdominal pain after bowel surgery is always neuropathic and unresponsive to any regional technique
choices.rationale: Postoperative deep abdominal cramping is predominantly visceral nociceptive pain, not neuropathic, and it can respond to techniques that cover visceral afferents such as a thoracic epidural.

choices.text: Local anesthetic from a TAP block is too rapidly absorbed to provide any meaningful duration of analgesia
choices.rationale: A TAP block provides hours of somatic analgesia, as evidenced by this patient's well-controlled incisional pain, so inadequate duration does not explain the persistent visceral pain.

explanation: The TAP block targets the somatic thoracolumbar nerves (approximately T6–L1) coursing in the plane between the internal oblique and transversus abdominis muscles, providing analgesia to the anterior and lateral abdominal wall and parietal peritoneum. A key, frequently tested limitation is that it provides somatic but not visceral analgesia. After bowel surgery, deep cramping mid-abdominal pain arises from visceral afferents and will not be covered by a TAP block; a technique that blocks visceral afferents, such as a thoracic epidural, is needed for that component.
subtopic: Truncal/peripheral nerve block coverage (TAP block)
reference_title: Transversus Abdominis Plane Block - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf

verdict.reason: Answer logic is sound and unambiguous. Keyed answer (index 1) — TAP block anesthetizes thoracolumbar somatic nerves (T6-L1) of the abdominal wall but does not block visceral afferents — is the single best explanation and is the well-established, frequently-tested limitation of the TAP block. The vignette is internally consistent: well-controlled somatic incisional pain plus persistent deep mid-abdominal cramping with nausea (classic visceral afferent pattern). All four distractors are genuinely incorrect/inferior: (0) a misplaced/subcutaneous injection would also degrade the somatic incisional analgesia, which is well controlled; (2) TAP works independently of intrathecal morphine; (3) postop cramping is visceral nociceptive, not neuropathic, and the absolutist 'always/any' is a giveaway; (4) TAP gives hours of analgesia, contradicted by the patient's controlled wound pain. No second defensible answer. Rationales and explanation are medically accurate and current. REFERENCE VERIFIED: NBK547730 resolves to 'Transversus Abdominis Plane Block - StatPearls' (Hammi & Ahn), exactly on-topic, and explicitly states the key point: 'A major limitation of the TAP block is that while it provides somatic analgesia, it fails to provide analgesia for visceral pain.' It also confirms the inter-muscular plane and T6-L1 coverage. Only nitpick (non-disqualifying): the explanation's claim of reliable 'parietal peritoneum' coverage is somewhat overstated/variable in the literature, but the keyed answer does not depend on it.

stem: A 24-year-old healthy woman receives a labor epidural. During placement using a loss-of-resistance technique with a 17-gauge Tuohy needle, there is an unintended dural puncture with free flow of cerebrospinal fluid. The epidural is resited at an adjacent interspace and labor proceeds uneventfully. On the second postpartum day she develops a bilateral fronto-occipital headache that is markedly worse when she sits or stands and is relieved within minutes of lying flat; she also reports mild neck stiffness and tinnitus. Her neurologic exam is otherwise normal and she is afebrile. Which feature of this case is most strongly associated with the highest risk of developing this complication?

choices.text: Her use of a labor epidural rather than a combined spinal-epidural technique
choices.rationale: The analgesic technique label itself does not determine risk; what matters is whether the dura is breached and the size and type of the needle that breaches it.

choices.text: Her supine positioning during epidural placement
choices.rationale: Patient positioning during placement is not an established major determinant of post-dural puncture headache risk.

choices.text: Her relatively low body mass index
choices.rationale: While obesity has a complex relationship with this complication, low BMI is not the dominant risk factor here compared with the large-bore dural breach.

choices.text: The fact that she is afebrile, which favors a low-pressure headache
choices.rationale: Absence of fever helps exclude infectious causes but is not itself a risk factor that increases the likelihood of developing the headache.

choices.text: Accidental dural puncture with a large-gauge cutting epidural (Tuohy) needle
choices.rationale: Larger-gauge needles and cutting (non-pencil-point) tips cause a bigger dural defect and greater cerebrospinal fluid leak, and an accidental wet tap with a 17-gauge Tuohy needle carries a high incidence of post-dural puncture headache.

explanation: This is a classic post-dural puncture headache (PDPH): a postural (worse upright, relieved supine) bilateral headache appearing within 5 days of a dural breach, often with neck pain, tinnitus, or other associated symptoms. The single most important risk factor is the dural puncture itself and, in particular, the needle: larger-gauge and cutting (Quincke/Tuohy) needles create a larger dural defect and greater cerebrospinal fluid leak than smaller pencil-point needles, so an accidental wet tap with a 17-gauge Tuohy needle confers a high PDPH incidence. Treatment ranges from conservative measures to a definitive epidural blood patch for severe or persistent cases.
subtopic: Post-dural puncture headache (neuraxial complication)
reference_title: Postdural Puncture Headache - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf

verdict.reason: Sound question. The case is a textbook PDPH (postural bilateral headache within 5 days of a witnessed dural breach, neck stiffness, tinnitus, normal exam, afebrile). The keyed answer (index 4) — accidental dural puncture with a large-gauge cutting Tuohy (17G) needle — is unambiguously the single best answer: large bore + cutting tip produces the largest dural defect and greatest CSF leak, and an accidental wet tap with a 17G Tuohy carries a very high PDPH incidence (~50-80%). This needle factor dominates all distractors. Distractors are all genuinely inferior/incorrect: technique label (epidural vs CSE) doesn't determine risk (breach does); supine positioning during placement is not an established risk factor; low BMI has at best a weak/complex association and is far inferior to the 17G cutting wet tap, and its rationale appropriately hedges; afebrile status is a diagnostic clue excluding infection, not a risk factor, and that option is the clearly worst. No two defensible answers, no wrong key. Rationales and explanation are medically accurate and current; characterizing Quincke and Tuohy as cutting needles is correct. REFERENCE VERIFIED: reference_url https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK430925/ resolves to the StatPearls 'Postdural Puncture Headache' chapter and is genuinely on-topic, confirming needle gauge and cutting vs pencil-point tip as risk factors and epidural blood patch as definitive treatment. No fabricated/irrelevant reference.

stem: A 68-year-old man with type 1 diabetes managed on NPH insulin is undergoing on-pump coronary artery bypass grafting. After successful separation from cardiopulmonary bypass, the surgeon requests heparin reversal and the perfusionist begins administering protamine 1 mg per 100 units of total heparin given. Approximately two minutes into the infusion, the systemic arterial pressure falls from 118/64 mmHg to 62/38 mmHg, the pulmonary artery pressure rises from 28/14 mmHg to 52/30 mmHg, central venous pressure climbs to 22 mmHg, and the right ventricle is seen to be acutely distended and hypokinetic on transesophageal echocardiography. Which of the following is the most appropriate immediate intervention?

choices.text: Stop the protamine infusion and treat with an inhaled or intravenous pulmonary vasodilator and inotropic/vasopressor support
choices.rationale: Correct: this presentation is a catastrophic (Type III) protamine reaction with pulmonary vasoconstriction and acute right heart failure, so halting protamine and supporting the right ventricle while lowering pulmonary pressures is the immediate priority.

choices.text: Continue the protamine infusion at a faster rate to fully neutralize circulating heparin
choices.rationale: Incorrect: faster protamine delivery worsens the reaction, and protamine itself, not residual heparin, is driving the hemodynamic collapse.

choices.text: Administer additional intravenous heparin and re-establish cardiopulmonary bypass before any other treatment
choices.rationale: Incorrect: although re-heparinization and returning to bypass may become necessary for refractory collapse, the first step is to stop the offending agent and attempt pharmacologic rescue of the right ventricle.

choices.text: Give a rapid 1 L crystalloid bolus to correct presumed hypovolemia
choices.rationale: Incorrect: the right ventricle is already distended with a high central venous pressure, so aggressive volume loading would further overdistend the failing RV and worsen output.

choices.text: Administer intravenous calcium chloride to reverse protamine-induced myocardial depression
choices.rationale: Incorrect: calcium does not address the pulmonary vasoconstriction and complement/thromboxane-mediated mechanism of a severe protamine reaction and will not relieve the RV afterload.

explanation: Severe (Type III) protamine reactions produce catastrophic pulmonary vasoconstriction with acute right ventricular pressure overload, systemic hypotension, and elevated pulmonary artery pressures, classically within minutes of administration. Patients on NPH insulin (which contains protamine) and those with fish allergy or prior protamine exposure are at higher risk. Immediate management is to stop the protamine, support the right ventricle with inotropes and vasopressors, and reduce RV afterload with a pulmonary vasodilator; re-heparinization and return to bypass are reserved for refractory cases.
subtopic: Protamine reaction / cardiopulmonary bypass separation

verdict.reason:
Verified against the reference (https://www.openanesthesia.org/keywords/protamine-reaction/), which resolves and is genuinely on-topic: it confirms the Type I/II/III classification, Type III as pulmonary vasoconstriction with RV failure, the risk factors (NPH insulin, fish allergy, prior protamine exposure), and the management cascade (pause protamine, vasoactive/inotropic support, pulmonary vasodilators, with re-heparinization/return to CPB reserved for refractory cases).

The scenario is a textbook catastrophic (Type III) protamine reaction: an NPH-insulin patient (recognized risk factor), onset within minutes of protamine, and acute RV distension/hypokinesis with high PAP, high CVP, and systemic hypotension — classic acute RV pressure overload from pulmonary vasoconstriction. The keyed answer (stop protamine + pulmonary vasodilator + inotrope/vasopressor support) is the single best, consensus first-line intervention.

Distractors are all genuinely inferior: B (continue/speed protamine) worsens the reaction; D (1 L bolus) overdistends an already-failing RV with CVP 22; E (calcium chloride) does not address pulmonary vasoconstriction or RV afterload. C (re-heparinize and return to bypass before any other treatment) is the strongest competitor but is explicitly reserved for refractory collapse — the word 'before any other treatment' correctly makes it inferior to first stopping the offending agent and attempting pharmacologic RV rescue. No two co-defensible answers.

Rationales and explanation are medically accurate and current. The 'complement/thromboxane-mediated' phrasing in the explanation is consistent with established protamine-reaction literature and is not an error. Reference is valid, on-topic, and supports the keyed answer.

stem: A 79-year-old woman with severe calcific aortic stenosis (valve area 0.7 cm2, mean gradient 52 mmHg) presents for hip fracture fixation under general anesthesia. Her baseline pressure is 138/82 mmHg and heart rate is 72 bpm in sinus rhythm. Shortly after induction with propofol, her heart rate increases to 124 bpm and is now an irregularly irregular rhythm on the monitor, with the blood pressure falling to 78/50 mmHg. Which of the following best explains why this rhythm is so poorly tolerated in this patient?

choices.text: The tachycardia raises myocardial oxygen demand while shortening diastolic coronary perfusion time to the hypertrophied left ventricle
choices.rationale: Incorrect alone: increased demand with reduced diastolic perfusion does contribute to ischemia, but it is not the single best explanation for the abrupt hemodynamic deterioration tied specifically to this rhythm change.

choices.text: Loss of the organized atrial contribution to filling of a stiff, hypertrophied left ventricle markedly reduces stroke volume across the fixed obstruction
choices.rationale: Correct: the noncompliant, hypertrophied ventricle in aortic stenosis is heavily dependent on the atrial kick, so the loss of organized atrial contraction in new atrial fibrillation sharply lowers preload, stroke volume, and cardiac output.

choices.text: Propofol has directly increased systemic vascular resistance, raising afterload across the stenotic valve
choices.rationale: Incorrect: propofol lowers, not raises, systemic vascular resistance, and a fixed valvular obstruction—not changing afterload—is the dominant problem.

choices.text: The increased rate has improved diastolic filling and caused pulmonary edema from volume overload
choices.rationale: Incorrect: tachycardia shortens diastolic filling time rather than improving it, and the acute picture is low output, not volume overload.

choices.text: Aortic stenosis converts the lesion into a volume-overload state in which any tachyarrhythmia causes acute mitral regurgitation
choices.rationale: Incorrect: aortic stenosis is a pressure-overload, not a primary volume-overload lesion, and acute mitral regurgitation is not the mechanism of decompensation here.

explanation: The hypertrophied, poorly compliant left ventricle of severe aortic stenosis depends heavily on the atrial kick for adequate end-diastolic filling, and on slower heart rates that preserve diastolic filling and coronary perfusion time. New-onset atrial fibrillation removes the organized atrial contribution while the rapid ventricular response shortens diastole, collapsing stroke volume across the fixed obstruction. Management favors maintaining sinus rhythm, avoiding tachycardia and hypotension, and prompt synchronized cardioversion when an acute arrhythmia causes hemodynamic instability.
subtopic: Valvular disease: aortic stenosis hemodynamic management
reference_title: Aortic Stenosis: Etiology, Pathophysiology, and Clinical Presentation (OpenAnesthesia)

verdict.reason:
Reference check PASSED: https://www.openanesthesia.org/keywords/aortic-stenosis/ resolves to a real, on-topic OpenAnesthesia page titled "Aortic Stenosis: Etiology, Pathophysiology, and Clinical Presentation" covering AS etiology/pathophysiology — a legitimate foundational reference for this topic (not fabricated or unrelated).

Answer is correct and unambiguous. The stem describes new-onset AFib with RVR (irregularly irregular rhythm) causing acute hypotension in severe AS. Keyed answer B (loss of organized atrial contribution to filling of a stiff, hypertrophied LV, reducing stroke volume across the fixed obstruction) is the classic, textbook-correct single best explanation: the noncompliant hypertrophied LV in severe AS is highly atrial-kick dependent, so losing it acutely collapses LVEDV/stroke volume and BP.

Adversarial check on distractor A (tachycardia raising O2 demand + shortening diastolic coronary perfusion): this statement is medically true and is a genuine contributor, which makes it a strong distractor — but it describes a demand/supply ischemia mechanism rather than the immediate cause of the abrupt drop in cardiac output. The defining feature flagged in the stem is the irregularly irregular rhythm (AFib), so loss of atrial kick is the single best answer. A's rationale correctly concedes its contribution while explaining why it is inferior. No two equally defensible answers.
